main_content:
This indicates that ATAT1 is the
acetyltransferase that responds to mechanical stress in melanoma
cells by stabilizing the tubulin cytoskeleton
context:
In addition to acetylation, MTs exhibit a variety of post-translational

and methylation, which influence MT stability25.
outcome:
To clarify the composi-
tion of the perinuclear tubulin network
predicted_context: It is well established that physical cues from the tumor microenvironment regulate phenotypic plasticity in cancer, and that cytoskeletal remodeling—such as the acetylation of microtubules—serves as a primary mechanotransduction mechanism to relay extracellular physical forces to the nucleus, thereby altering chromatin states.
predicted_outcome: Therefore, this suggests that ATAT1-driven tubulin stabilization acts as the critical mechanosensory bridge linking physical microenvironmental confinement to the epigenetic reprogramming required for melanoma invasion and progression.

main_content:
To clarify the composi-
tion of the perinuclear tubulin network
outcome:
we used immunofluorescence
to characterize MT PTMs in confined melanoma cells.
predicted_context: The microtubule cytoskeleton exhibits significant molecular diversity driven by post-translational modifications (PTMs), commonly referred to as the 'tubulin code'. Modifications such as acetylation and detyrosination act as established markers for stable, long-lived microtubules. Under physical stress or mechanical confinement, these modified tubulin populations are known to preferentially organize into specialized structural networks around the nucleus to provide mechanical resistance and regulate nuclear positioning. Profiling these structural subpopulations using PTM-specific antibodies is an established approach to mapping cytoskeletal organization.
predicted_outcome: We will perform multiplexed immunofluorescence staining on melanoma cells cultured in confined microenvironments using antibodies specific to total α-tubulin, acetylated α-tubulin, and detyrosinated α-tubulin, followed by high-resolution confocal microscopy and image quantification to map the precise subpopulation makeup of the perinuclear microtubules.

main_content:
However, most MTs in confined and
unconfined A375 cells were highly tyrosinated (Extended Data Fig. 4a–d), ...
Although confined perinuclear MTs were occasionally
tyrosinated and rarely detyrosinated (Extended Data Fig. 4a–d), there
was no specific enrichment of these PTMs in the perinuclear network,
as observed for acetylated MTs. Similar results were observed for poly-
glutamylated MTs (Extended Data Fig. 4e,f). No polyglycylated MTs
were found in either condition (Extended Data Fig. 4g,h).
context:
Detyrosination
has been linked to stabilized MTs26 ... probably owing to the high concentrations of tyrosine and tyrosinase in melanoma27
outcome:
This indicates
that acetylation is the primary PTM mediating the assembly and/or
stability of the perinuclear tubulin network.
predicted_context: Microtubule post-translational modifications (PTMs) act as a 'tubulin code' that regulates distinct biomechanical properties, stability, and protein interactions of the cytoskeleton, meaning specialized cellular structures typically rely on specific subsets of modified microtubules.
predicted_outcome: This indicates that the formation and function of the perinuclear microtubule network during mechanical confinement is a highly specific structural adaptation driven uniquely by microtubule acetylation, rather than a general accumulation or non-specific reorganization of various microtubule modifications.

main_content:
Our results indicate that confinement induces a neuronal identity in
interface melanoma cells, characterized by changes in cytoskeletal
architecture and gene expression.
outcome:
We examined our zebrafish and
human transcriptomic datasets to identify potential confinement-
induced mediators of this state.
predicted_context: Melanoma cells possess profound intrinsic phenotypic plasticity owing to their embryonic neural crest origin, enabling them to de-differentiate and adopt distinct transcriptional states—including neural-like or invasive identities—in response to external cues. Mechanical spatial confinement within the tumor microenvironment is an established physical stressor that triggers mechanotransduction pathways, heavily remodeling the cytoskeleton and altering downstream gene expression networks. Previous studies have established that single-cell transcriptomic profiling combined with structural imaging can successfully map these state transitions and structural adaptations in melanoma models.
predicted_outcome: Perform single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) on melanoma cells cultured in engineered mechanically confined microenvironments versus unconfined controls to quantify the upregulation of neuronal gene expression signatures, and conduct immunofluorescence staining for cytoskeletal elements (e.g., F-actin) and neuronal markers (e.g., beta-III tubulin/TUBB3), using confocal microscopy to visualize and quantify architectural remodeling.

main_content:
In invading tumour cells, HMGB2
was only upregulated in elongated or misshaped tumour cells that
appeared to be under mechanical pressure from adjacent tissues
(Fig. 3e–g),
outcome: indicating that confinement induces HMGB2 upregulation
predicted_context: It is well established that mechanical cues from the tumour microenvironment can alter cellular morphology and induce stable changes in chromatin architecture to drive phenotypic plasticity in cancer cells.
predicted_outcome: This indicates that mechanical confinement directly triggers the upregulation of HMGB2, suggesting that this protein acts as a key molecular effector linking physical squeezing by the microenvironment to the chromatin reorganization necessary for melanoma cells to transition into an invasive state.

main_content:
Using time-lapse
confocal microscopy of A375 cells stably expressing HMGB2–GFP, we
found nuclear HMGB2–GFP concentrations increased linearly over
approximately 16 h (0.269 ± 0.020-fold per hour) to a final concentra-
tion of 1.76 ± 0.072-fold relative to initial concentrations (Extended
Data Fig. 5a,b). We also examined the concentrations of HMG family
members HMGB1 and HMGA1, which were transcriptionally upregu-
lated in interface cells to a lesser extent than HMGB2, and quantified no
change in their expression upon confinement (Extended Data Fig. 5c–f),
outcome:
indicating that confinement-induced upregulation of HMGB2 is not a
general property of all HMG family members.
predicted_context: It is established that mechanical cues from the tumour microenvironment can induce stable changes in chromatin architecture to drive phenotypic plasticity, an active process that relies on the precise regulation of specific chromatin-associated architectural proteins rather than a generic cellular stress response.
predicted_outcome: This indicates that the accumulation of HMGB2 is a highly specific response to mechanical confinement, rather than a generalized upregulation of all HMG family members, suggesting that HMGB2 specifically mediates the chromatin remodeling required for melanoma cells to transition into an invasive state.

main_content:
As in the zebrafish, the
invasive front in human samples often contained elongated nuclei
with high HMGB2 and acetylated tubulin expression, although with
expected interpatient variability (Extended Data Fig. 6a–e). Of the 40
patient samples analysed, 20 (50%) contained putative interface cells
(elongated nuclei, HMGB2+ and AcTub+), nine (22.5%) contained cells
exhibiting perinuclear acetylated tubulin enrichment only (AcTub+ and
HMGB2−), three (7.5%) contained cells with elongated HMGB2+ nuclei
but no acetylated tubulin (AcTub− and HMGB2+) and the remaining eight (20%) showed no enrichment of HMGB2 or acetylated tubulin
(Extended Data Fig. 6d,e).
outcome:
This validates our scRNA-seq analyses,
indicating that interface cells are present in human samples (Fig. 1c).
predicted_context: These findings are consistent with the experimental observations in the zebrafish model, where the combination of elongated nuclei, elevated HMGB2, and acetylated tubulin (AcTub) serve as reliable cellular markers for melanoma cells undergoing a mechanically confined, invasive phenotypic transition at the tumor-microenvironment interface.
predicted_outcome: This indicates that the mechanical confinement-induced transition to an invasive cell state is a conserved and clinically relevant mechanism in human melanoma, demonstrating that microenvironment-driven phenotypic plasticity is a key feature of human disease progression rather than an organism-specific phenomenon.

main_content:
We then investigated whether the upregulation of HMGB2 and acety-
lated tubulin by confined cells is melanoma-specific or exhibited across
other cancer types.
outcome:
We focused on two cancer types probably influ-
enced by mechanical stress in vivo: pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma
and bladder cancer.
predicted_context: To determine whether mechanobiological responses are conserved across distinct lineages, researchers standardly employ controlled in vitro confinement assays on a diverse panel of cell lines derived from different tissue origins. Devices such as polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) cell confiners or microchannels allow for the precise application of uniform spatial restriction (e.g., compressing cells to a 3-micron height). Such platforms are highly established tools for mimicking the physical constraints of dense tumor microenvironments and have been utilized broadly to reveal conserved cellular responses to mechanical stress, such as cytoskeletal reorganization and nuclear deformation, across various solid tumors.
predicted_outcome: We applied an in vitro PDMS-based mechanical cell confiner to subject a broad panel of non-melanoma human cancer cell lines (including breast, colorectal, prostate, and lung cancer cells) to uniform physical compression. Following confinement, we performed Western blotting and immunofluorescence to quantify the protein expression levels and localization of HMGB2 and acetylated tubulin compared to unconfined control cells of the same lineages.

main_content:
To rule
out a potential pro-invasive role for these transcription factors upon
mechanical stress in melanoma,
context:
Mechanical stress can be associated with nuclear translocation of
transcription factors, such as YAP, as well as epithelial–mesenchymal
transition inducers, including Twist, Snail and SMAD3 (ref. 30).
outcome:
we quantified their expression in confined
A375 cells.
predicted_context: Mechanosensitive transcription factors (such as YAP and TAZ) and canonical epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT) regulators (such as ZEB1 and SNAI1) are widely established drivers of cancer cell invasion in response to physical forces in the tumor microenvironment. To confirm that the observed phenotypic transition is driven primarily by mechanical confinement-induced chromatin architecture alterations rather than these classical mechanotransduction pathways, it is necessary to assess whether these transcription factors are upregulated, activated, or functionally required for invasion under these specific stress conditions.
predicted_outcome: we quantified their mRNA and protein expression levels using RT-qPCR and Western blot analysis, assessed their nuclear translocation (activation state) via immunofluorescence staining, and performed siRNA-mediated knockdown to verify that mechanically confined melanoma cells still successfully transition to an invasive state even in their absence.

main_content:
To investigate how mechanical confinement induces HMGB2 upregu-
lation,
outcome:
we focused on the MT cytoskeleton, hypothesizing that the
perinuclear acetylated tubulin network (Fig. 2f,g) may propagate
confinement-induced force to the nucleus.
predicted_context: Mechanical confinement imposes physical constraints on both the cell and its nucleus, which can induce rapid changes in chromatin architecture and subsequent gene expression. Established mechanobiological principles demonstrate that physical forces transmitted to the nucleus can directly alter heterochromatin condensation and accessibility, thereby driving mechanosensitive transcription (Tajik et al., 2016). To delineate the epigenetic modifications governing this process, previous studies utilize the Assay for Transposase-Accessible Chromatin using sequencing (ATAC-seq), a robust and highly sensitive method to map genome-wide changes in open chromatin and identify active regulatory elements under specific stimuli (Buenrostro et al., 2013). Applying this approach allows us to pinpoint whether physical squeezing opens specific enhancer or promoter regions associated with the target gene.
predicted_outcome: we will subject cultured melanoma cells to in vitro mechanical confinement using a microfluidic cell confiner device and subsequently perform ATAC-seq (Assay for Transposase-Accessible Chromatin using sequencing) paired with RNA-seq. This will allow us to map confinement-induced shifts in chromatin accessibility at the HMGB2 locus and perform motif enrichment analysis to identify the mechanosensitive transcription factors responsible for its transcriptional activation.

main_content:
To confirm that the perinu-
clear network is upstream of HMGB2 enrichment in the confinement
response,
outcome:
we generated stable A375 cell lines in which HMGB2 was inac-
tivated using CRISPR (Extended Data Fig. 8a–c).
predicted_context: The perinuclear network, predominantly consisting of perinuclear actin and the LINC (Linker of Nucleoskeleton and Cytoskeleton) complex, physically couples the cellular cytoskeleton to the nucleus. This structural connection is established as essential for transmitting mechanical forces from the extracellular microenvironment (such as physical confinement) directly to the chromatin, triggering nuclear deformation, chromatin remodeling, and the recruitment of architectural proteins. To functionally establish causality and demonstrate that a signaling component is 'upstream' in mechanotransduction pathways, standard experimental designs involve uncoupling this structural linkage to see if the downstream effect is lost. Previous studies have robustly demonstrated that overexpressing a dominant-negative KASH domain (DN-KASH) or pharmacologically depolymerizing actin prevents force transmission to the nucleus and abolishes downstream mechanically-induced transcriptional and structural responses.
predicted_outcome: We will disrupt the perinuclear structural network in melanoma cells by transfecting them with a dominant-negative KASH (DN-KASH) construct (to uncouple the LINC complex) or treating them with an actin-depolymerizing agent such as Latrunculin A. Following this disruption, we will subject the cells to mechanical confinement using an established in vitro tunable agarose overlay assay or microchannel device. We will then perform subcellular fractionation and quantitative immunofluorescence coupled with confocal microscopy to measure the extent of HMGB2 enrichment at the chromatin/nucleus, comparing the response of these functionally uncoupled cells to control-confined cells.

main_content:
to determine whether the acetylated tubulin network influences
HMGB2 upregulation.
context:
Tubulin acetylation is mediated by the acetyl-
transferase ATAT1 (ref. 31) and deacetylation by HDAC6 (ref. 32).
outcome:
We modulated tubulin dynamics and acetylation state in confined
cells ... We first treated A375 cells with the HDAC6 inhibitor tubacin33,
predicted_context: The microtubule cytoskeleton, specifically the acetylation of alpha-tubulin at lysine 40, provides structural resilience and acts as a critical mechanosensor that transduces physical cues from the microenvironment into intracellular signaling pathways. Mechanical confinement alters microtubule dynamics, which can subsequently transmit forces to the nucleus to induce changes in chromatin architecture and gene expression. Modulating tubulin acetylation levels through genetic or pharmacological targeting of the primary tubulin acetyltransferase, alpha-TAT1 (also known as MEC-17), or the tubulin deacetylase, HDAC6, is a well-established strategy to evaluate the dependence of downstream mechanotransduction targets on the acetylated microtubule network.
predicted_outcome: We will culture melanoma cells under in vitro mechanical confinement using a micro-pillar or constrained gel overlay system. To perturb the acetylated tubulin network, we will either perform siRNA-mediated knockdown of alpha-TAT1 to deplete acetylation or treat the cells with the HDAC6 inhibitor Tubastatin A to hyper-acetylate the network. Subsequently, we will quantify HMGB2 mRNA and protein levels using RT-qPCR and Western blotting, respectively, to assess whether the disruption of tubulin acetylation prevents or alters the confinement-induced upregulation of HMGB2.

main_content: To stabilize MTs independent of acetylation,
context:
which binds β-tubulin and prevents tubulin
monomer incorporation into MT filaments and thus should not
influence the acetylation state of HMGB2 or other proteins34.
outcome:
we treated confined cells
with paclitaxel (Taxol),
predicted_context: Microtubule (MT) stability is often correlated with post-translational modifications, particularly α-tubulin acetylation at Lys40 by the enzyme ATAT1. However, to functionally uncouple MT structural stability from the acetylation mark itself, microtubules can be pharmacologically stabilized using agents like paclitaxel (Taxol). Paclitaxel binds directly to the β-tubulin subunit within the microtubule lumen, lowering the critical concentration of tubulin required for assembly and actively preventing depolymerization. This mechanism physically locks the microtubule polymer in a stable state biochemically, completely bypassing the physiological requirement for stabilizing post-translational modifications.
predicted_outcome: we treated the melanoma cells with the pharmacological microtubule-stabilizing agent paclitaxel (Taxol).

main_content:
Taxol
(100 nM) significantly increased HMGB2 accumulation in confined
cells (P = 0.00230; Extended Data Fig. 9g,h), with an accumulation
rate remarkably similar to tubacin-treated cells (0.338 ± 0.0425-fold
per hour versus 0.353 ± 0.0364-fold per hour; P = 0.961; Extended Data
Fig. 9i).
outcome:
Although we cannot rule out off-target effects of tubacin on
HMGB2, similar results in Taxol-treated and tubacin-treated cells indi-
cate that confinement-induced HMGB2 enrichment is linked to MT
stability, rather than acetylation state or other HDAC6 functions.
predicted_context: It is well established that both Taxol and tubacin perturb microtubule dynamics; Taxol directly stabilizes microtubules, whereas tubacin acts as an HDAC6 inhibitor that leads to increased microtubule acetylation and subsequent stabilization.
predicted_outcome: This indicates that microtubule stabilization and decreased cytoskeletal flexibility are the mechanistic drivers of HMGB2 accumulation during mechanical confinement, linking environment-induced cytoskeletal alterations to the chromatin remodelling required for melanoma phenotypic plasticity.

main_content:
To confirm the role of perinuclear acetylated tubulin in mediating
HMGB2 accumulation
context:
Nocodazole abolishes most MT
networks, with the exception of stabilized acetylated tubulin filaments24.
outcome:
we treated A375[HMGB2–GFP] cells with noco-
dazole before applying confinement.
predicted_context: Alpha-tubulin acetylation, primarily catalyzed by the acetyltransferase ATAT1 (also known as MEC-17), is a critical post-translational modification that regulates microtubule dynamics, intracellular transport, and mechanotransduction. Genetically depleting ATAT1 is a well-established method to selectively abolish tubulin acetylation without disrupting the overall microtubule network, allowing researchers to isolate the specific role of acetylated microtubules in the spatial distribution and nuclear accumulation of target proteins.
predicted_outcome: Perform siRNA-mediated knockdown or CRISPR-Cas9 knockout of the acetyltransferase ATAT1 in mechanically confined melanoma cells, followed by immunofluorescence staining and confocal microscopy to quantify the resulting changes in perinuclear and nuclear HMGB2 localization.

main_content:
We quantified HMGB2 accumulation in confined A375–
ATAT1KO cells lacking acetylated tubulin (Extended Data Fig. 3c–e). Unexpectedly, HMGB2 accumulation was not impaired in ATAT1KO
cells (Extended Data Fig. 3f),
outcome:
suggesting that acetylated tubulin is
sufficient but not necessary for the enrichment of HMGB2 in response
to confinement.
predicted_context: Microtubules and their post-translational modifications, specifically acetylation mediated by ATAT1, are typically expected to play a critical role in cellular mechanosensing and translating mechanical stress into intracellular responses.
predicted_outcome: This indicates that the mechanical confinement-induced accumulation of HMGB2 is driven by a distinct mechanotransduction pathway that functions independently of ATAT1-mediated microtubule acetylation.

main_content:
Thus, we investigated other factors that may cooperate with the MT
cytoskeleton to promote upregulation of HMGB2 in confined cells
context: TurboID35
outcome:
using
TurboID35 to perform proximity labelling proteomics targeting HMGB2
interactors (Fig. 3l and Supplementary Table 4).
predicted_context: Mechanotransduction and cellular adaptation to physical confinement frequently rely on the functional crosstalk between the microtubule network and the actomyosin cytoskeleton. Established biological principles demonstrate that microtubules intimately cooperate with actin filaments and myosin-driven contractility to transmit external mechanical forces to the nucleus, which subsequently alters chromatin architecture and transcriptional profiles.
predicted_outcome: We subjected melanoma cells to mechanical confinement using a specialized microchannel device and treated them with specific pharmacological inhibitors targeting the actomyosin network (e.g., Blebbistatin to inhibit Myosin II, and Latrunculin A to disrupt actin polymerization). We then quantified the resulting HMGB2 mRNA and protein levels via RT-qPCR and Western blotting to determine if actin contractility is required alongside the MT cytoskeleton for HMGB2 upregulation.

main_content:
One highly enriched
protein was nesprin 2 (gene name: SYNE2) ... (Fig. 3m and Supplemen-
tary Table 4).
context:
a component of the linker
of nucleoskeleton and cytoskeleton (LINC) complex that connects the
cytoskeleton, nuclear lamina and chromatin36
outcome:
We hypothesized that nesprin 2 may be required for enrich-
ment of HMGB2 upon confinement.
predicted_context: It is well established that nesprin 2 is a core component of the LINC (Linker of Nucleoskeleton and Cytoskeleton) complex, which physically tethers the cytoskeleton to the nuclear envelope and is critical for transmitting extracellular mechanical forces directly to the nucleus and chromatin.
predicted_outcome: This indicates that nesprin 2 acts as the primary mechanotransducer that relays mechanical confinement cues from the tumour microenvironment to the nucleus, thereby driving the chromatin architecture remodeling required for melanoma cells to transition into an invasive state.

main_content:
Accordingly, confined A375 cells
upregulated nesprin 2 (Extended Data Fig. 10a,b), and targeting SYNE2
with short interfering RNA (siRNA) (Extended Data Fig. 10c) abolished
the confinement-mediated accumulation of HMGB2 (Fig. 3n,o) and
perinuclear tubulin network (Fig. 3p,q).
outcome:
This indicates that nesprin 2
and the tubulin cytoskeleton interact to upregulate HMGB2.
predicted_context: It is well established that nesprin 2 (encoded by the SYNE2 gene) is a key component of the LINC (Linker of Nucleoskeleton and Cytoskeleton) complex, which physically couples the cytoskeleton to the nuclear envelope and is critical for transmitting external mechanical forces into the nucleus (mechanotransduction).
predicted_outcome: Therefore, this indicates that nesprin 2 is mechanistically required to transduce external mechanical confinement signals from the microenvironment into the nucleus, thereby driving the intracellular restructuring and chromatin-related changes (HMGB2 accumulation) necessary for melanoma phenotypic plasticity.

main_content:
One highly enriched
protein was nesprin 2 (gene name: SYNE2), a component of the linker
of nucleoskeleton and cytoskeleton (LINC) complex that connects the
cytoskeleton, nuclear lamina and chromatin36
context:
The LINC complex cooperates with the nuclear lamina to tune
nuclear stiffness36.
outcome: N/A
predicted_context: It is well established that the LINC complex acts as a crucial mechanotransduction bridge, transmitting physical forces from the extracellular environment and cytoskeleton directly to the nucleus to regulate nuclear morphology and chromatin organization.
predicted_outcome: This indicates that nesprin 2 and the LINC complex play a primary role in transducing the external forces of mechanical confinement into the stable alterations in chromatin architecture that drive the transition of melanoma cells into an invasive state.

main_content:
Lamin A/C increased by approximately 3-fold in
confined cells (P = 1.28 × 10−162; Extended Data Fig. 10d,e)
outcome: suggesting that the nuclear lamina was remodelled in response to confinement,
predicted_context: It is well established that Lamin A/C is a mechanosensitive structural protein of the nuclear envelope whose expression scales with tissue stiffness and physical stress, acting to regulate nuclear deformability and protect genomic integrity during confined migration.
predicted_outcome: This indicates that mechanical confinement actively triggers a structural remodeling of the nucleus in melanoma cells, providing the necessary mechanical adaptation to withstand physical microenvironmental stress and facilitating the phenotypic transition to an invasive state.

main_content: suggesting that the nuclear lamina was remodelled in response to confinement
outcome: which we validated by means of atomic force microscopy (AFM)
predicted_context: The nucleus functions as a primary cellular mechanosensor. Established mechanobiological principles demonstrate that physical spatial confinement directly deforms the nucleus, stretching the nuclear envelope and triggering structural remodeling of the nuclear lamina. This adaptive remodeling is typically characterized by the spatial redistribution, morphological wrinkling, or compositional shifts of structural proteins such as Lamin A/C and Lamin B1 to accommodate mechanical stress and modulate chromatin architecture.
predicted_outcome: Culture melanoma cells under mechanical confinement using custom microfluidic devices with restricted heights (e.g., 3 μm) versus unconfined controls, and perform immunofluorescence staining for Lamin A/C and Lamin B1, followed by high-resolution confocal microscopy to quantify changes in lamina wrinkling, localization, and overall nuclear envelope morphology.

main_content:
suggesting that the nuclear lamina was remodelled in response to confinement which we validated by means of atomic force microscopy (AFM)
showing increased nuclear stiffness in confined cells (Extended Data
Fig. 10f,g).
outcome:
Together, our results demonstrate that confined melanoma
cells remodel cytoskeletal and nuclear structures to reinforce the cell
against mechanical force, resulting in LINC complex-mediated HMGB2
upregulation and nuclear stiffening.
predicted_context: It is well established that the nuclear lamina acts as a crucial mechanosensor that dictates nuclear biomechanics, and its structural remodeling in response to physical forces is a recognized mechanism for altering nuclear stiffness and influencing chromatin organization.
predicted_outcome: This indicates that mechanical confinement induces a direct biophysical adaptation in the melanoma cell nucleus, altering its rigidity and serving as the mechanotransduction trigger for the chromatin architectural changes required to drive the transition toward an invasive phenotype.

main_content:
Our results indicate that the LINC complex and tubulin cytoskel-
eton cooperate to stabilize the nucleus against mechanical stress
and upregulate HMGB2.
context:
HMGB2 typically binds chromatin without
sequence specificity and bends DNA to relieve mechanical strain37.
outcome:
Confinement-induced nuclear shape changes could thus increase
the strain on chromatin and create a densely packed nuclear envi-
ronment that affects HMGB2 dynamics.
predicted_context: It is well established that the LINC (Linker of Nucleoskeleton and Cytoskeleton) complex physically couples the cytoskeleton to the nucleus to transmit mechanical cues from the microenvironment (mechanotransduction), and architectural proteins like HMGB2 are known to restructure chromatin to regulate widespread transcriptional changes.
predicted_outcome: This indicates that mechanical confinement triggers a distinct mechanosensory pathway via the LINC-tubulin axis, driving HMGB2-mediated chromatin remodeling to facilitate the stable phenotypic transition of melanoma cells from a proliferative to an invasive state.

main_content:
Confinement-induced nuclear shape changes could thus increase
the strain on chromatin and create a densely packed nuclear envi-
ronment that affects HMGB2 dynamics.
outcome:
We used fluorescence recov-
ery after photobleaching (FRAP) to characterize nuclear HMGB2
dynamics upon confinement.
predicted_context: Fluorescence Recovery After Photobleaching (FRAP) is a well-established live-cell imaging technique used to quantify the intracellular mobility and binding dynamics of chromatin-associated proteins. By measuring the recovery rate of a bleached fluorophore, FRAP reveals the diffusion coefficient and the bound fraction of proteins, effectively acting as an indicator of how physical crowding and chromatin compaction restrict protein movement. Previous studies have successfully utilized FRAP of GFP-tagged HMGB proteins to demonstrate how changes in global chromatin architecture and density directly alter their nuclear diffusion rates and interaction dynamics.
predicted_outcome: Transfect melanoma cells with a fluorescently labeled HMGB2 construct (e.g., GFP-HMGB2), subject them to mechanical confinement using a cell-confiner device or confined microchannels, and perform live-cell FRAP assays on the deformed nuclei to measure the recovery kinetics and mobile fraction of HMGB2 compared to unconfined control cells.

main_content:
Our FRAP analysis indicates that confinement upregulates HMGB2 and
stabilizes its interactions with chromatin; .
outcome:
therefore, we propose that this
could affect chromatin accessibility
predicted_context: It is well established that high-mobility group box (HMGB) proteins act as key architectural chromatin factors, and changes in their structural binding dynamics are indicative of global chromatin remodeling and subsequent transcriptional reprogramming.
predicted_outcome: This suggests that HMGB2 acts as a critical molecular mediator that translates mechanical confinement into stable alterations in chromatin architecture, thereby driving the transcriptomic reprogramming necessary for melanoma cells to transition into an invasive phenotype.

main_content:
confinement upregulates HMGB2 and

outcome:
Assay for transposase-accessible
chromatin (ATAC) sequencing (ATAC-seq) of A375 cells overexpressing
HMGB2
predicted_context: To investigate whether the stabilization of the architectural protein HMGB2 on chromatin under mechanical confinement alters the global chromatin landscape, a highly sensitive method for mapping open chromatin is required. The Assay for Transposase-Accessible Chromatin using sequencing (ATAC-seq) is a widely established technique that utilizes a hyperactive mutant Tn5 transposase to selectively insert sequencing adapters into sterically accessible (open) regions of the genome. This allows for high-resolution, genome-wide profiling of chromatin accessibility changes driven by structural proteins and microenvironmental cues.
predicted_outcome: Perform ATAC-seq on melanoma cells cultured in unconfined versus mechanically confined microenvironments, as well as on confined melanoma cells with and without HMGB2 depletion (e.g., using CRISPR-Cas9 or siRNA), to quantify genome-wide changes in chromatin accessibility dependent on HMGB2.

main_content:
Homer de novo motif analysis
identified conserved transcription factor binding motifs enriched within the promoter of open chromatin regions upon HMGB2 overex-
pression (HMGB2OE). The top-ranked motif was an AP-1 motif (Fig. 4k;
P = 1 × 10−49) ...
Other highly ranked motifs included PRDM4, ... (Fig. 4k; P = 1 × 10−12), and SOX9, ... (Fig. 4k; P = 1 × 10−11).
context:
...implicated in melanoma and melanocyte plasticity40,41... which functions in neural
development42 ... a critical regulator of
the pro-invasive phenotype switch in melanoma43
outcome:
These data indicate that HMGB2 upregulation increases chromatin
accessibility at loci associated with plasticity and a neuronal pheno-
type, promoting a confinement-induced dedifferentiation program.
predicted_context: It is well established that transcription factors such as AP-1 and SOX9 are master regulators of cellular plasticity in melanoma, specifically functioning to drive the transcriptional programs required for the transition from a proliferative state to a highly invasive, mesenchymal-like state.
predicted_outcome: This indicates that HMGB2 mediates the transition to an invasive melanoma phenotype by structurally remodeling chromatin to increase the accessibility of target promoters for AP-1 and SOX9, thereby facilitating the activation of invasion-driving transcriptional networks.

main_content: To clarify potential HMGB2 targets
context: a double-crosslinking approach44
outcome:
we performed chromatin immu-
noprecipitation (ChIP) sequencing (ChIP–seq) targeting HMGB2 in
A375 cells using a double-crosslinking approach44 (Extended Data
Fig. 11a). We generated a peak atlas by comparing peaks present in cells
expressing baseline concentrations of HMGB2 to those not present in
HMGB2KO cells
predicted_context: High Mobility Group Box 2 (HMGB2) is a non-histone chromatin architectural protein that dynamically modulates transcription by physically interacting with the genome. To systematically identify the direct downstream targets of chromatin-binding proteins, established methodologies employ Chromatin Immunoprecipitation followed by high-throughput sequencing (ChIP-seq) to map genomic occupancy. When coupled with transcriptomic profiling (RNA-seq) following target depletion, researchers can reliably distinguish direct structural targets from indirect downstream regulatory effects.
predicted_outcome: Perform Chromatin Immunoprecipitation sequencing (ChIP-seq) using an anti-HMGB2 antibody on melanoma cells subjected to mechanically confining conditions to map its direct genomic binding sites. Concurrently, perform RNA sequencing (RNA-seq) on identically confined melanoma cells following siRNA- or shRNA-mediated HMGB2 knockdown, and intersect the datasets to isolate genes that are both directly bound and transcriptionally regulated by HMGB2.

main_content:
After manual filtering to remove intergenic,
low-quality or non-specific peaks, we identified a final high-confidence
set of 96 targets (Extended Data Fig. 11b and Supplementary Table 6).
We observed HMGB2 binding at promoter regions of several AP-1 sig-
nalling genes, including FOSL1, JUNB and JUND (Extended Data Fig. 11c
and Supplementary Table 6),
outcome:
validating our ATAC-seq results and sup-
porting the pro-invasive role of HMGB2.
predicted_context: It is well established that the AP-1 transcription factor complex (which includes FOSL1, JUNB, and JUND) is a key driver of the invasive, drug-resistant phenotype in melanoma, and that HMGB2 functions as a chromatin architectural protein that facilitates transcriptional regulation by modifying DNA structure.
predicted_outcome: This indicates that HMGB2 directly regulates the expression of AP-1 complex members at the chromatin level, suggesting a mechanism wherein mechanical confinement induces structural chromatin changes that recruit HMGB2 to these specific promoters, thereby driving the melanoma cells' phenotypic transition into an invasive state.

HMGB2OE cells adopted a mesenchymal-like and invasive morphology
relative to empty vector controls (Extended Data Fig. 11g). The most
highly upregulated gene was UNC5D ... (Extended Data Fig. 11h and Supplemen-

UNC5D, a netrin receptor promoting neu-
ronal survival and migration47

subsection: HMGB2 drives invasion through Notch and BRN2
main_content:
Phenotypic plasticity in melanoma is controlled by a regulatory axis of melanocytic transcription factors (MITF) driving proliferation versus invasive factors (BRN2, SOX9 and AP-1) that also slow proliferation. We investigated whether HMGB2 drives invasion through these fac-
tors.
outcome:
Using the SKMEL5 melanocytic cell line, we created a stable line
expressing V5-tagged HMGB2 and performed ChIP–seq targeting V5
and HMGB2 (Extended Data Fig. 12a).
predicted_context: To establish whether a putative upstream regulator coordinates a known transcriptional network, standard experimental paradigms utilize targeted genetic depletion of the regulator followed by quantification of downstream targets. Previous foundational studies characterizing the MITF and BRN2/SOX9 plasticity axis in melanoma have successfully employed this strategy—using siRNA-mediated knockdown and subsequent immunoblotting or transcript profiling—to demonstrate how specific upstream nodes govern the transition between proliferative (MITF-high) and invasive (BRN2/SOX9-high) states. Therefore, manipulating HMGB2 expression and measuring the reciprocal changes in these specific transcription factors is a robust method to determine its position within this regulatory hierarchy.
predicted_outcome: We performed siRNA-mediated knockdown of HMGB2 in melanoma cell lines and subsequently conducted Western blot and RT-qPCR analyses to measure the resulting protein and mRNA expression levels of MITF, BRN2, SOX9, and AP-1.

main_content: Confined A375 cells downregulated many proliferation-related pathways. To visualize the influence of confinement on this trade-off,
context: FastFUCCI cell cycle sensor54
outcome:
we generated
an A375 cell line stably expressing the FastFUCCI cell cycle sensor54.
predicted_context: Phenotypic switching in melanoma is well-characterized by a distinct trade-off between actively proliferating and invasive/migratory states. To dynamically monitor such shifts in cell cycle status, the FUCCI (Fluorescent Ubiquitination-based Cell Cycle Indicator) system is widely utilized. It leverages the cell-cycle-dependent ubiquitination and degradation of specific proteins (Cdt1 in S/G2/M phases and Geminin in G1 phase) conjugated to different fluorophores, enabling the real-time visualization of proliferative arrest and cell-cycle distribution at single-cell resolution within mechanically restricted microenvironments.
predicted_outcome: we transduced A375 melanoma cells with the FUCCI cell-cycle reporter system and conducted time-lapse live-cell fluorescence microscopy to monitor their cell cycle dynamics and spatial distribution while subjected to mechanical confinement in custom microfluidic channels.

main_content:
Upon confinement, all initially mitotic cells (mAG+ and S/G2–M phase)
rapidly lost mAG fluorescence
outcome: indicating cell cycle exit (Fig. 5c,d).
predicted_context: It is well established in fluorescent cell cycle reporter systems that mAG expression specifically marks cells actively progressing through the proliferative S/G2 and M phases, and the loss of this fluorescent signal is indicative of cell cycle arrest or an exit from active division.
predicted_outcome: This indicates that mechanical confinement directly and rapidly triggers cell cycle arrest, forcing the melanoma cells to abandon their actively proliferative state, which serves as a crucial initial step in their phenotypic transition toward an invasive state.

main_content: To directly test the role of HMGB2 in melanoma invasion
outcome:
we used
in vitro invasion assays (Fig. 5e)
predicted_context: Phenotypic plasticity and the transition to an invasive state in melanoma are heavily governed by chromatin remodeling and structural proteins. To directly establish the functional necessity of specific chromatin-associated proteins in cancer cell invasion, it is standard scientific practice to employ targeted genetic depletion (such as CRISPR-Cas9 or shRNA) coupled with a physiological invasion assay. The zebrafish xenograft model is an established, highly tractable system widely used for monitoring human melanoma cell invasion and dissemination in vivo, as the optical transparency of the embryos allows for high-resolution tracking of invasive behavior within a live microenvironment.
predicted_outcome: We will deplete HMGB2 in human melanoma cell lines using CRISPR-Cas9 knockout or shRNA knockdown, and then perform an in vivo invasion assay by microinjecting the fluorescently labeled engineered cells into zebrafish embryos to track and quantify their dissemination and tissue invasion relative to control cells.

Loss

These data indicate that HMGB2 is required for the invasive state, and

predicted_outcome: This indicates that hmgb2 and hmgb2b function as critical drivers of phenotypic plasticity in melanoma, acting to suppress tumor proliferation while promoting the transition to an invasive state.

HMGB family proteins contain three functional domains: A-box and

predicted_context: It is well established that HMGB proteins function as architectural chromatin factors, where the A-box and B-box domains intercalate into the minor groove of DNA to induce bending and structural remodeling, while the acidic tail modulates these interactions and binds to histones.
predicted_outcome: Therefore, this specific structural configuration indicates that HMGB proteins are mechanistically equipped to serve as key chromatin remodelers, facilitating the large-scale changes in chromatin architecture that drive the transition of melanoma cells to an invasive state in response to mechanical confinement.

predicted_context: It is a well-established principle in cancer biology that specific molecular domains or mechanosensitive pathways are often uniquely responsible for mediating specialized adaptive responses to microenvironmental cues (such as mechanical confinement) without being essential for baseline cell viability, gross cytoskeletal structure, or basic oncogenic proliferation.
predicted_outcome: This demonstrates that the targeted deletion constructs do not cause general cellular toxicity, impair basic tumor progression, or inherently disrupt basal microtubule stability in vivo. Consequently, it confirms that these specific protein domains function specifically to mediate confinement-induced phenotypic plasticity and mechanotransduction, uncoupled from general tumor growth.

subsection: HMGB2 is associated with drug tolerance
main_content:
We examined how the confined invasive state affects
therapeutic response, hypothesizing that the HMGB2-high neuronal
state induced by confinement may promote drug tolerance
outcome:
To test
this, we treated confined cells with Taxol to stabilize MTs.
predicted_context: Phenotypic plasticity is a well-established mechanism of acquired drug resistance in melanoma. Specifically, the transition of melanoma cells into de-differentiated, neural crest-like, or 'neuronal' transcriptional states allows them to evade targeted therapies, such as MAPK pathway inhibitors. Prior studies have demonstrated that exposing melanoma cells to BRAF and MEK inhibitors enriches for these rare, drug-tolerant persister cells exhibiting neural signatures. Because mechanical confinement mimics the physical constraints of the tumor microenvironment and induces this HMGB2-high neuronal state, applying standard-of-care targeted therapies to mechanically confined cells is a logical approach to determine if physical cues alone can orchestrate drug-resistant phenotypes.
predicted_outcome: We cultured patient-derived human melanoma cells and established cell lines (e.g., A375) in both unconfined environments and mechanically confined systems (such as microfluidic confinement devices or under-agarose assays). We then treated both populations with standard-of-care targeted therapies (the BRAF inhibitor vemurafenib and the MEK inhibitor trametinib) and evaluated therapeutic tolerance by quantifying cell viability and apoptosis using CellTiter-Glo assays and Annexin V/PI flow cytometry.

predicted_outcome: This demonstrates that mechanical confinement acts as a critical microenvironmental trigger that directly confers chemoresistance, protecting melanoma cells from microtubule-targeting chemotherapeutics like Taxol.

subsection: HMGB2 is associated with drug tolerance
main_content: We then tested the role of HMGB2 in drug tolerance in vivo
context:
dabrafenib and
trametinib, widely used targeted therapies in melanoma61 (Fig. 5n).
outcome:
by
transplanting human melanoma cells overexpressing HMGB2 into
athymic mice and treating the resulting tumours with dabrafenib and
trametinib
predicted_context: Drug tolerance in melanoma is frequently driven by non-genetic phenotypic plasticity, mediated by dynamic changes in chromatin architecture in response to the tumor microenvironment. To establish a causal relationship between specific chromatin-associated proteins (such as HMGB2) and drug resistance in a physiological setting, researchers conventionally employ genetic ablation of the target gene followed by drug efficacy testing in animal models. Utilizing patient-derived or cell-line xenografts in zebrafish or murine models allows for the longitudinal observation of tumor regression and survival during exposure to standard targeted therapeutics, such as BRAF inhibitors, demonstrating whether the loss of the gene resensitizes the tumor to the drug.
predicted_outcome: We generated HMGB2-knockout melanoma cell lines using CRISPR-Cas9, xenografted them into our in vivo zebrafish model, and treated the engrafted animals with the BRAF inhibitor vemurafenib. We then longitudinally measured tumor volume and survival rates, comparing the drug response of the HMGB2-depleted xenografts to that of control-transduced tumors.

subsection: HMGB2 is associated with drug tolerance
main_content:
HMGB2 overexpression significantly impaired melanoma response to
dabrafenib/trametinib in vivo (P = 0.043; Fig. 5o, Extended Data Fig. 13e
and Supplementary Table 10).
outcome:
Together, our results support a role for
confinement-mediated upregulation of HMGB2 inducing a pro-invasive
and drug-tolerant state in melanoma (Extended Data Fig. 15).
predicted_context: It is well established that melanoma cells can develop resistance to targeted therapies, such as the MAPK inhibitors dabrafenib and trametinib, through phenotypic plasticity and global changes in chromatin architecture rather than acquiring new genetic mutations.
predicted_outcome: This indicates that HMGB2 acts as a functional driver of therapy resistance, suggesting its role in mediating the chromatin structural changes required for melanoma cells to transition into and maintain a drug-resistant phenotypic state.
